Shrine|en|Kamayama Shrine|fr|Sanctuaire Kamayama|ja|竈山神社|simple|Kamayama Shrine|WD=Q11597743}} {{commons category|Category:Shizuhi-jinja}} [[JA:静火神社]] {{wikidata link|Q28693031}} == Japanese content == {{Shinto shrine |Name = Shizuhi Shrine |Image = [[File:Shizuhi-jinja.JPG|250px]]
Torii gate and shrine buildings |Location = Wada, {{jalink|Wakayama Prefecture|Q131314}}{{jalink|Wakayama City|Q200747}} |Coordinates= {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|12|10.64|N|135|12|28.86|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Shizuhi Shrine}} |Deity = Shizuhi Ōkami |Shintai = |Rank = {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} ({{jalink|Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964|Myōjin Grand Shrine}})
Former {{jalink|Village Shrine|Q134917284}}
An extramural subsidiary shrine of {{jalink|Kamayama Shrine|Q11597743}} |Founded = Unknown
(before {{jalink|Jōwa (Japan)|Q1075605|Jōwa}} 11 ({{jalink|844|Q23835}})) |Main Hall = |Alternate names = |Pilgrimage site = Ki Sansho-sha |Annual festival = {{jalink|April 16|Q2519}} |Rites = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Wakayama|label=Shizuhi Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=220}} }} {{Coordinate list}} [[File:Shizuhi-jinja kyuuchi.JPG|thumb|250px|right|Former site monument of Shizuhi Shrine (foreground) and Mount Yakushi, where the current shrine stands]] '''Shizuhi Shrine''' (しずひじんじゃ / しづひじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} located in Wada, {{jalink|Wakayama Prefecture|Q131314}}{{jalink|Wakayama City|Q200747}}. It is a {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} ({{jalink|Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964}}), and under the {{jalink|modern system of shrine ranks|Q712534|former shrine ranking system}} it was classified as a {{jalink|Village Shrine|Q134917284}}. Today, it is an extramural subsidiary shrine of {{jalink|Kamayama Shrine|Q11597743}}. Together with {{jalink|Date Shrine (Wakayama City)|Q11380847|Date Shrine}} (Sonobe, Wakayama City) and {{jalink|Shima Shrine|Q17212913}} ({{jalink|Nakanoshima (Wakayama City)|Q17226981|Nakanoshima}}, Wakayama City), it is known as the “Ki Sansho-sha” (Three Shrines of Ki) {{Sfn|Shizuhi Shrine Site (Heibonsha)|1983|p=376}}. === Enshrined deity === The enshrined deity is the following single kami {{Sfn|Shizuhi Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987|p=99}}: :* Shizuhi Ōkami According to documents of the Ugai clan, the shrine’s hereditary priests, the deity was identified as {{jalink|Kagutsuchi|Q1640658|Hi-no-Musubi no Kami}} {{Sfn|Shizuhi Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987|p=99}}. Another theory, found in the *Zoku Fudoki*, holds that the “Ki Sansho-sha” were branch enshrinements of the three deities of Isonokami—Itsu no Take no Mikoto, Ōyatsuhime no Mikoto, and Tsumatsuhime no Mikoto—and identifies the deity of Shizuhi Shrine as Tsumatsuhime no Mikoto {{Sfn|Shizuhi Shrine Site (Heibonsha)|1983|p=376}}. Yet another view considers Shizuhi to have been a “fire-quelling deity” of Kii Province, known as the “Land of Trees” {{Sfn|Kamayama Shrine and Shizuhi Shrine (Kami-gami)|1986|pp=316–318}}. In addition, the *{{jalink|Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki|Q11381815}}* states that Funatama Shrine, a subsidiary shrine of {{jalink|Sumiyoshi Taisha|Q705949}}, was the clan deity of the Ki clan and the main shrine of “Shima Shrine, Shizuhi Shrine, and Date Shrine.” === History === === Founding === The date of founding is unknown. Originally, the shrine stood about 200 meters east of its current location ({{Coord|34|12|9.46|N|135|12|37.07|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|name=Former site of Shizuhi Shrine}}) {{Sfn|Shizuhi Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987|p=99}}. According to the *Zoku Fudoki*, Shizuhi Shrine had fallen into ruin before the {{jalink|Einin|Q1327307}} era ({{jalink|1293|Q5557}}–{{jalink|1299|Q5564}}), but a document of the Hinokuma Shrine from {{jalink|Chōkyō|Q968843}} 1 ({{jalink|1487|Q6652}}) records that one *to* of ritual rice was allocated to Shizuhi Shrine {{Sfn|Shizuhi Shrine Site (Heibonsha)|1983|p=376}}. According to the *Nanki Tokugawa-shi*, by around {{jalink|Kyōhō|Q1145048}} 8 ({{jalink|1723|Q6921}}) the shrine had again fallen into ruin; in that same year, Wakayama Domain rebuilt the shrine buildings on Mount Yakushi (Maeyama / Amagiri-yama; the present site), and Kamayama Shrine assumed its administration {{Sfn|Shizuhi Shrine Site (Heibonsha)|1983|p=376}}. === Overview === In the national histories, together with Date Shrine and Shima Shrine (Shima Jinja), Shizuhi Shrine is recorded as having received promotions in divine rank: from Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade to Senior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade in {{jalink|Jōwa (Japan)|Q1075605|Jōwa}} 11 ({{jalink|844|Q23835}}); to Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade in {{jalink|Kashō|Q1373157}} 3 ({{jalink|850|Q30364}}); to Senior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade in {{jalink|Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|Jōgan}} 1 ({{jalink|859|Q23897}}); and to Junior Third Rank in Jōgan 17 ({{jalink|875|Q23816}}) {{Sfn|Shizuhi Shrine Site (Heibonsha)|1983|p=376}}. In the *{{jalink|Engishiki|Q1342448}}* *{{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|Register of Deities}}*, it is listed under {{jalink|Kii Province|Q864053}}{{jalink|Nagusa District|Q11415547}} as “Shizuhi Shrine, Myōjin Grand Shrine,” placing it among the {{jalink|Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964}}; the *{{jalink|Kii no Kuni Jinmyōchō|Q17210008}}* records it as “Senior First Rank, Shizuhi Ōkami” {{Sfn|Shizuhi Shrine Site (Heibonsha)|1983|p=376}}. In all cases, it is ranked higher than Kamayama Shrine. It is said that together with {{jalink|Date Shrine (Wakayama City)|Q11380847|Date Shrine}} (Sonobe, Wakayama City) and {{jalink|Shima Shrine|Q17212913}} ({{jalink|Nakanoshima (Wakayama City)|Q17226981|Nakanoshima}}, Wakayama City), it was known as the “Ki Sansho-sha” {{Sfn|Shima Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983|p=362}}. References to the Ki Sansho-sha appear beginning with a rice storage ledger from {{jalink|Eishō|Q1193549}} 3 ({{jalink|1048|Q19359}}), as well as in the *{{jalink|Chūyūki|Q11363231}}* entry for {{jalink|Tennen|Q1193599}} 2 ({{jalink|1109|Q19515}}) and in the four-verse sacred songs of the *{{jalink|Ryōjin Hishō|Q3010584}}* {{Sfn|Shima Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983|p=362}}. According to the *{{jalink|Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki|Q11381815}}*, the origin of the Ki Sansho-sha lies in the three ships used by {{jalink|Empress Jingū|Q232803}} for the {{jalink|conquest of the Three Kingdoms of Korea|Q11249435}}, which she had {{jalink|Takenouchi no Sukune|Q1073668}} enshrine {{Sfn|Shima Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983|p=362}}. Furthermore, since Wada Village was formerly a shrine estate of {{jalink|Hinokuma Shrine and Kunikakasu Shrine|Q888184}}, the deity of Shizuhi was closely connected with those shrines and with the {{jalink|Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Q11604953|hereditary governors of Kii Province}}. At Kusa-no-miya, enshrined within the grounds of those shrines, it is said that on September 15 the deity of Shizuhi made a sacred procession and the Shizuhi Festival was held {{Sfn|Kamayama Shrine and Shizuhi Shrine (Kami-gami)|1986|pp=316–318}}. In {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}} 6 ({{jalink|1873|Q7757}}), under the {{jalink|modern system of shrine ranks|Q712534}}, it was designated a {{jalink|Village Shrine|Q134917284}}; however, in {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}} 42 ({{jalink|1909|Q2057}}) it was merged into Inari Shrine, a subsidiary shrine of Kamayama Shrine {{Sfn|Shizuhi Shrine Site (Heibonsha)|1983|p=376}}{{Sfn|Shizuhi Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987|p=99}}. In {{jalink|Shōwa|Q327567}} 25 ({{jalink|1950|Q18597}}), shrine buildings were constructed on the original Mount Yakushi site and the deity was transferred there {{Sfn|Shizuhi Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987|p=99}}. === Divine ranks === * Records of divine rank promotions in the Six National Histories ** {{jalink|Jōwa (Japan)|Q1075605|Jōwa}} 11 ({{jalink|844|Q23835}}) 11th month, 3rd day: from Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade to Senior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade (*{{jalink|Shoku Nihon Kōki|Q1041301}}*) – recorded as “Shizuhi no Kami.” ** {{jalink|Kashō|Q1373157}} 3 ({{jalink|850|Q30364}}) 10th month, 21st day: Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade (*{{jalink|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|Q1052905}}*) – recorded as “Shizuhi no Kami.” ** {{jalink|Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|Jōgan}} 1 ({{jalink|859|Q23897}}) 1st month, 27th day: from Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade to Senior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade (*{{jalink|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Q1052981}}*) – recorded as “Shizuhi no Kami.” ** Jōgan 17 ({{jalink|875|Q23816}}) 10th month, 17th day: from Senior Fourth Rank, Upper Grade to Junior Third Rank (*Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku*) – recorded as “Shizuhi no Kami.” * After the Six National Histories ** Senior First Rank (*{{jalink|Kii no Kuni Jinmyōchō|Q17210008}}*) – recorded as “Shizuhi Ōkami.” === Notes === {{Footnote help}} {{reflist}} === References Prefecture|publisher={{jalink|Heibonsha|Q11482108}}|isbn=458249031X|ref=}} ** {{Wikicite|reference="Shizuhi Shrine Site"|ref={{Harvid|Shizuhi Shrine Site (Heibonsha)|1983}}}}, {{Wikicite|reference="Shima Shrine"|ref={{Harvid|Shima Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}}}. * {{Cite book|Japanese|editor={{jalink|Kenichi Tanigawa|Q2392596}} (ed.)|author=Maruyama Akinori|year=1986|chapter=Kamayama Shrine and Shizuhi Shrine|title=Gods of Japan – Shrines and Sacred Places – Vol. 6 Ise, Shima, Iga, Kii|publisher={{jalink|Hakusuisha|Q4346470}}|isbn=456002216X|ref={{Harvid|Kamayama Shrine and Shizuhi Shrine (Kami-gami)|1986}}}} * {{Cite book|Japanese|editor=Association for Shikinaisha Studies (ed.)|author=Kiyoshi Nakano|chapter=Shizuhi Shrine|year=1987|title=Report on Shikinaisha Surveys, Vol. 23 Nankaidō|publisher={{jalink|Kōgakkan University|Q1797584}} Press|page=|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Shizuhi Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987}}}} === Related items === * {{jalink|Kamayama Shrine|Q11597743}} {{Shinto sidebar}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Shizuhijinja}} == Categories == Shrine|en|Kamayama Shrine|fr|Sanctuaire Kamayama|ja|竈山神社|simple|Kamayama Shrine|WD=Q11597743}} {{commons category|Category:Shizuhi-jinja}} [[JA:静火神社]] {{wikidata link|Q28693031}} == Japanese content == {{Shinto shrine |Name = Shizuhi Shrine |Image = [[File:Shizuhi-jinja.JPG|250px]]
Torii gate and shrine buildings |Location = Wada, {{jalink|Wakayama Prefecture|Q131314}}{{jalink|Wakayama City|Q200747}} |Coordinates= {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|12|10.64|N|135|12|28.86|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Shizuhi Shrine}} |Deity = Shizuhi Ōkami |Shintai = |Rank = {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} ({{jalink|Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964|Myōjin Grand Shrine}})
Former {{jalink|Village Shrine|Q134917284}}
An extramural subsidiary shrine of {{jalink|Kamayama Shrine|Q11597743}} |Founded = Unknown
(before {{jalink|Jōwa (Japan)|Q1075605|Jōwa}} 11 ({{jalink|844|Q23835}})) |Main Hall = |Alternate names = |Pilgrimage site = Ki Sansho-sha |Annual festival = {{jalink|April 16|Q2519}} |Rites = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Wakayama|label=Shizuhi Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=220}} }} {{Coordinate list}} [[File:Shizuhi-jinja kyuuchi.JPG|thumb|250px|right|Former site monument of Shizuhi Shrine (foreground) and Mount Yakushi, where the current shrine stands]] '''Shizuhi Shrine''' (しずひじんじゃ / しづひじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} located in Wada, {{jalink|Wakayama Prefecture|Q131314}}{{jalink|Wakayama City|Q200747}}. It is a {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} ({{jalink|Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964}}), and under the {{jalink|modern system of shrine ranks|Q712534|former shrine ranking system}} it was classified as a {{jalink|Village Shrine|Q134917284}}. Today, it is an extramural subsidiary shrine of {{jalink|Kamayama Shrine|Q11597743}}. Together with {{jalink|Date Shrine (Wakayama City)|Q11380847|Date Shrine}} (Sonobe, Wakayama City) and {{jalink|Shima Shrine|Q17212913}} ({{jalink|Nakanoshima (Wakayama City)|Q17226981|Nakanoshima}}, Wakayama City), it is known as the “Ki Sansho-sha” (Three Shrines of Ki) {{Sfn|Shizuhi Shrine Site (Heibonsha)|1983|p=376}}. === Enshrined deity === The enshrined deity is the following single kami {{Sfn|Shizuhi Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987|p=99}}: :* Shizuhi Ōkami According to documents of the Ugai clan, the shrine’s hereditary priests, the deity was identified as {{jalink|Kagutsuchi|Q1640658|Hi-no-Musubi no Kami}} {{Sfn|Shizuhi Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987|p=99}}. Another theory, found in the *Zoku Fudoki*, holds that the “Ki Sansho-sha” were branch enshrinements of the three deities of Isonokami—Itsu no Take no Mikoto, Ōyatsuhime no Mikoto, and Tsumatsuhime no Mikoto—and identifies the deity of Shizuhi Shrine as Tsumatsuhime no Mikoto {{Sfn|Shizuhi Shrine Site (Heibonsha)|1983|p=376}}. Yet another view considers Shizuhi to have been a “fire-quelling deity” of Kii Province, known as the “Land of Trees” {{Sfn|Kamayama Shrine and Shizuhi Shrine (Kami-gami)|1986|pp=316–318}}. In addition, the *{{jalink|Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki|Q11381815}}* states that Funatama Shrine, a subsidiary shrine of {{jalink|Sumiyoshi Taisha|Q705949}}, was the clan deity of the Ki clan and the main shrine of “Shima Shrine, Shizuhi Shrine, and Date Shrine.” === History === === Founding === The date of founding is unknown. Originally, the shrine stood about 200 meters east of its current location ({{Coord|34|12|9.46|N|135|12|37.07|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|name=Former site of Shizuhi Shrine}}) {{Sfn|Shizuhi Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987|p=99}}. According to the *Zoku Fudoki*, Shizuhi Shrine had fallen into ruin before the {{jalink|Einin|Q1327307}} era ({{jalink|1293|Q5557}}–{{jalink|1299|Q5564}}), but a document of the Hinokuma Shrine from {{jalink|Chōkyō|Q968843}} 1 ({{jalink|1487|Q6652}}) records that one *to* of ritual rice was allocated to Shizuhi Shrine {{Sfn|Shizuhi Shrine Site (Heibonsha)|1983|p=376}}. According to the *Nanki Tokugawa-shi*, by around {{jalink|Kyōhō|Q1145048}} 8 ({{jalink|1723|Q6921}}) the shrine had again fallen into ruin; in that same year, Wakayama Domain rebuilt the shrine buildings on Mount Yakushi (Maeyama / Amagiri-yama; the present site), and Kamayama Shrine assumed its administration {{Sfn|Shizuhi Shrine Site (Heibonsha)|1983|p=376}}. === Overview === In the national histories, together with Date Shrine and Shima Shrine (Shima Jinja), Shizuhi Shrine is recorded as having received promotions in divine rank: from Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade to Senior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade in {{jalink|Jōwa (Japan)|Q1075605|Jōwa}} 11 ({{jalink|844|Q23835}}); to Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade in {{jalink|Kashō|Q1373157}} 3 ({{jalink|850|Q30364}}); to Senior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade in {{jalink|Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|Jōgan}} 1 ({{jalink|859|Q23897}}); and to Junior Third Rank in Jōgan 17 ({{jalink|875|Q23816}}) {{Sfn|Shizuhi Shrine Site (Heibonsha)|1983|p=376}}. In the *{{jalink|Engishiki|Q1342448}}* *{{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|Register of Deities}}*, it is listed under {{jalink|Kii Province|Q864053}}{{jalink|Nagusa District|Q11415547}} as “Shizuhi Shrine, Myōjin Grand Shrine,” placing it among the {{jalink|Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964}}; the *{{jalink|Kii no Kuni Jinmyōchō|Q17210008}}* records it as “Senior First Rank, Shizuhi Ōkami” {{Sfn|Shizuhi Shrine Site (Heibonsha)|1983|p=376}}. In all cases, it is ranked higher than Kamayama Shrine. It is said that together with {{jalink|Date Shrine (Wakayama City)|Q11380847|Date Shrine}} (Sonobe, Wakayama City) and {{jalink|Shima Shrine|Q17212913}} ({{jalink|Nakanoshima (Wakayama City)|Q17226981|Nakanoshima}}, Wakayama City), it was known as the “Ki Sansho-sha” {{Sfn|Shima Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983|p=362}}. References to the Ki Sansho-sha appear beginning with a rice storage ledger from {{jalink|Eishō|Q1193549}} 3 ({{jalink|1048|Q19359}}), as well as in the *{{jalink|Chūyūki|Q11363231}}* entry for {{jalink|Tennen|Q1193599}} 2 ({{jalink|1109|Q19515}}) and in the four-verse sacred songs of the *{{jalink|Ryōjin Hishō|Q3010584}}* {{Sfn|Shima Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983|p=362}}. According to the *{{jalink|Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki|Q11381815}}*, the origin of the Ki Sansho-sha lies in the three ships used by {{jalink|Empress Jingū|Q232803}} for the {{jalink|conquest of the Three Kingdoms of Korea|Q11249435}}, which she had {{jalink|Takenouchi no Sukune|Q1073668}} enshrine {{Sfn|Shima Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983|p=362}}. Furthermore, since Wada Village was formerly a shrine estate of {{jalink|Hinokuma Shrine and Kunikakasu Shrine|Q888184}}, the deity of Shizuhi was closely connected with those shrines and with the {{jalink|Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Q11604953|hereditary governors of Kii Province}}. At Kusa-no-miya, enshrined within the grounds of those shrines, it is said that on September 15 the deity of Shizuhi made a sacred procession and the Shizuhi Festival was held {{Sfn|Kamayama Shrine and Shizuhi Shrine (Kami-gami)|1986|pp=316–318}}. In {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}} 6 ({{jalink|1873|Q7757}}), under the {{jalink|modern system of shrine ranks|Q712534}}, it was designated a {{jalink|Village Shrine|Q134917284}}; however, in {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}} 42 ({{jalink|1909|Q2057}}) it was merged into Inari Shrine, a subsidiary shrine of Kamayama Shrine {{Sfn|Shizuhi Shrine Site (Heibonsha)|1983|p=376}}{{Sfn|Shizuhi Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987|p=99}}. In {{jalink|Shōwa|Q327567}} 25 ({{jalink|1950|Q18597}}), shrine buildings were constructed on the original Mount Yakushi site and the deity was transferred there {{Sfn|Shizuhi Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987|p=99}}. === Divine ranks === * Records of divine rank promotions in the Six National Histories ** {{jalink|Jōwa (Japan)|Q1075605|Jōwa}} 11 ({{jalink|844|Q23835}}) 11th month, 3rd day: from Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade to Senior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade (*{{jalink|Shoku Nihon Kōki|Q1041301}}*) – recorded as “Shizuhi no Kami.” ** {{jalink|Kashō|Q1373157}} 3 ({{jalink|850|Q30364}}) 10th month, 21st day: Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade (*{{jalink|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|Q1052905}}*) – recorded as “Shizuhi no Kami.” ** {{jalink|Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|Jōgan}} 1 ({{jalink|859|Q23897}}) 1st month, 27th day: from Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade to Senior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade (*{{jalink|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Q1052981}}*) – recorded as “Shizuhi no Kami.” ** Jōgan 17 ({{jalink|875|Q23816}}) 10th month, 17th day: from Senior Fourth Rank, Upper Grade to Junior Third Rank (*Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku*) – recorded as “Shizuhi no Kami.” * After the Six National Histories ** Senior First Rank (*{{jalink|Kii no Kuni Jinmyōchō|Q17210008}}*) – recorded as “Shizuhi Ōkami.” === Notes === {{Footnote help}} {{reflist}} === References Commons category (P373) == * Shizuhi-jinja == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '和歌山県和歌山市和田', 'language': 'ja'} == parent organization or unit (P749) == * {{ill|Kamayama Shrine|lt=Kamayama Shrine|en|Kamayama Shrine|fr|Sanctuaire Kamayama|ja|竈山神社|simple|Kamayama Shrine|WD=Q11597743}} {{commons category|Category:Shizuhi-jinja}} [[JA:静火神社]] {{wikidata link|Q28693031}} == Japanese content == {{Shinto shrine |Name = Shizuhi Shrine |Image = [[File:Shizuhi-jinja.JPG|250px]]
Torii gate and shrine buildings |Location = Wada, {{jalink|Wakayama Prefecture|Q131314}}{{jalink|Wakayama City|Q200747}} |Coordinates= {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|12|10.64|N|135|12|28.86|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Shizuhi Shrine}} |Deity = Shizuhi Ōkami |Shintai = |Rank = {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} ({{jalink|Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964|Myōjin Grand Shrine}})
Former {{jalink|Village Shrine|Q134917284}}
An extramural subsidiary shrine of {{jalink|Kamayama Shrine|Q11597743}} |Founded = Unknown
(before {{jalink|Jōwa (Japan)|Q1075605|Jōwa}} 11 ({{jalink|844|Q23835}})) |Main Hall = |Alternate names = |Pilgrimage site = Ki Sansho-sha |Annual festival = {{jalink|April 16|Q2519}} |Rites = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Wakayama|label=Shizuhi Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=220}} }} {{Coordinate list}} [[File:Shizuhi-jinja kyuuchi.JPG|thumb|250px|right|Former site monument of Shizuhi Shrine (foreground) and Mount Yakushi, where the current shrine stands]] '''Shizuhi Shrine''' (しずひじんじゃ / しづひじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} located in Wada, {{jalink|Wakayama Prefecture|Q131314}}{{jalink|Wakayama City|Q200747}}. It is a {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} ({{jalink|Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964}}), and under the {{jalink|modern system of shrine ranks|Q712534|former shrine ranking system}} it was classified as a {{jalink|Village Shrine|Q134917284}}. Today, it is an extramural subsidiary shrine of {{jalink|Kamayama Shrine|Q11597743}}. Together with {{jalink|Date Shrine (Wakayama City)|Q11380847|Date Shrine}} (Sonobe, Wakayama City) and {{jalink|Shima Shrine|Q17212913}} ({{jalink|Nakanoshima (Wakayama City)|Q17226981|Nakanoshima}}, Wakayama City), it is known as the “Ki Sansho-sha” (Three Shrines of Ki) {{Sfn|Shizuhi Shrine Site (Heibonsha)|1983|p=376}}. === Enshrined deity === The enshrined deity is the following single kami {{Sfn|Shizuhi Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987|p=99}}: :* Shizuhi Ōkami According to documents of the Ugai clan, the shrine’s hereditary priests, the deity was identified as {{jalink|Kagutsuchi|Q1640658|Hi-no-Musubi no Kami}} {{Sfn|Shizuhi Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987|p=99}}. Another theory, found in the *Zoku Fudoki*, holds that the “Ki Sansho-sha” were branch enshrinements of the three deities of Isonokami—Itsu no Take no Mikoto, Ōyatsuhime no Mikoto, and Tsumatsuhime no Mikoto—and identifies the deity of Shizuhi Shrine as Tsumatsuhime no Mikoto {{Sfn|Shizuhi Shrine Site (Heibonsha)|1983|p=376}}. Yet another view considers Shizuhi to have been a “fire-quelling deity” of Kii Province, known as the “Land of Trees” {{Sfn|Kamayama Shrine and Shizuhi Shrine (Kami-gami)|1986|pp=316–318}}. In addition, the *{{jalink|Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki|Q11381815}}* states that Funatama Shrine, a subsidiary shrine of {{jalink|Sumiyoshi Taisha|Q705949}}, was the clan deity of the Ki clan and the main shrine of “Shima Shrine, Shizuhi Shrine, and Date Shrine.” === History === === Founding === The date of founding is unknown. Originally, the shrine stood about 200 meters east of its current location ({{Coord|34|12|9.46|N|135|12|37.07|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|name=Former site of Shizuhi Shrine}}) {{Sfn|Shizuhi Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987|p=99}}. According to the *Zoku Fudoki*, Shizuhi Shrine had fallen into ruin before the {{jalink|Einin|Q1327307}} era ({{jalink|1293|Q5557}}–{{jalink|1299|Q5564}}), but a document of the Hinokuma Shrine from {{jalink|Chōkyō|Q968843}} 1 ({{jalink|1487|Q6652}}) records that one *to* of ritual rice was allocated to Shizuhi Shrine {{Sfn|Shizuhi Shrine Site (Heibonsha)|1983|p=376}}. According to the *Nanki Tokugawa-shi*, by around {{jalink|Kyōhō|Q1145048}} 8 ({{jalink|1723|Q6921}}) the shrine had again fallen into ruin; in that same year, Wakayama Domain rebuilt the shrine buildings on Mount Yakushi (Maeyama / Amagiri-yama; the present site), and Kamayama Shrine assumed its administration {{Sfn|Shizuhi Shrine Site (Heibonsha)|1983|p=376}}. === Overview === In the national histories, together with Date Shrine and Shima Shrine (Shima Jinja), Shizuhi Shrine is recorded as having received promotions in divine rank: from Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade to Senior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade in {{jalink|Jōwa (Japan)|Q1075605|Jōwa}} 11 ({{jalink|844|Q23835}}); to Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade in {{jalink|Kashō|Q1373157}} 3 ({{jalink|850|Q30364}}); to Senior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade in {{jalink|Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|Jōgan}} 1 ({{jalink|859|Q23897}}); and to Junior Third Rank in Jōgan 17 ({{jalink|875|Q23816}}) {{Sfn|Shizuhi Shrine Site (Heibonsha)|1983|p=376}}. In the *{{jalink|Engishiki|Q1342448}}* *{{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|Register of Deities}}*, it is listed under {{jalink|Kii Province|Q864053}}{{jalink|Nagusa District|Q11415547}} as “Shizuhi Shrine, Myōjin Grand Shrine,” placing it among the {{jalink|Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964}}; the *{{jalink|Kii no Kuni Jinmyōchō|Q17210008}}* records it as “Senior First Rank, Shizuhi Ōkami” {{Sfn|Shizuhi Shrine Site (Heibonsha)|1983|p=376}}. In all cases, it is ranked higher than Kamayama Shrine. It is said that together with {{jalink|Date Shrine (Wakayama City)|Q11380847|Date Shrine}} (Sonobe, Wakayama City) and {{jalink|Shima Shrine|Q17212913}} ({{jalink|Nakanoshima (Wakayama City)|Q17226981|Nakanoshima}}, Wakayama City), it was known as the “Ki Sansho-sha” {{Sfn|Shima Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983|p=362}}. References to the Ki Sansho-sha appear beginning with a rice storage ledger from {{jalink|Eishō|Q1193549}} 3 ({{jalink|1048|Q19359}}), as well as in the *{{jalink|Chūyūki|Q11363231}}* entry for {{jalink|Tennen|Q1193599}} 2 ({{jalink|1109|Q19515}}) and in the four-verse sacred songs of the *{{jalink|Ryōjin Hishō|Q3010584}}* {{Sfn|Shima Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983|p=362}}. According to the *{{jalink|Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki|Q11381815}}*, the origin of the Ki Sansho-sha lies in the three ships used by {{jalink|Empress Jingū|Q232803}} for the {{jalink|conquest of the Three Kingdoms of Korea|Q11249435}}, which she had {{jalink|Takenouchi no Sukune|Q1073668}} enshrine {{Sfn|Shima Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983|p=362}}. Furthermore, since Wada Village was formerly a shrine estate of {{jalink|Hinokuma Shrine and Kunikakasu Shrine|Q888184}}, the deity of Shizuhi was closely connected with those shrines and with the {{jalink|Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Q11604953|hereditary governors of Kii Province}}. At Kusa-no-miya, enshrined within the grounds of those shrines, it is said that on September 15 the deity of Shizuhi made a sacred procession and the Shizuhi Festival was held {{Sfn|Kamayama Shrine and Shizuhi Shrine (Kami-gami)|1986|pp=316–318}}. In {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}} 6 ({{jalink|1873|Q7757}}), under the {{jalink|modern system of shrine ranks|Q712534}}, it was designated a {{jalink|Village Shrine|Q134917284}}; however, in {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}} 42 ({{jalink|1909|Q2057}}) it was merged into Inari Shrine, a subsidiary shrine of Kamayama Shrine {{Sfn|Shizuhi Shrine Site (Heibonsha)|1983|p=376}}{{Sfn|Shizuhi Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987|p=99}}. In {{jalink|Shōwa|Q327567}} 25 ({{jalink|1950|Q18597}}), shrine buildings were constructed on the original Mount Yakushi site and the deity was transferred there {{Sfn|Shizuhi Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987|p=99}}. === Divine ranks === * Records of divine rank promotions in the Six National Histories ** {{jalink|Jōwa (Japan)|Q1075605|Jōwa}} 11 ({{jalink|844|Q23835}}) 11th month, 3rd day: from Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade to Senior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade (*{{jalink|Shoku Nihon Kōki|Q1041301}}*) – recorded as “Shizuhi no Kami.” ** {{jalink|Kashō|Q1373157}} 3 ({{jalink|850|Q30364}}) 10th month, 21st day: Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade (*{{jalink|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|Q1052905}}*) – recorded as “Shizuhi no Kami.” ** {{jalink|Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|Jōgan}} 1 ({{jalink|859|Q23897}}) 1st month, 27th day: from Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade to Senior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade (*{{jalink|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Q1052981}}*) – recorded as “Shizuhi no Kami.” ** Jōgan 17 ({{jalink|875|Q23816}}) 10th month, 17th day: from Senior Fourth Rank, Upper Grade to Junior Third Rank (*Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku*) – recorded as “Shizuhi no Kami.” * After the Six National Histories ** Senior First Rank (*{{jalink|Kii no Kuni Jinmyōchō|Q17210008}}*) – recorded as “Shizuhi Ōkami.” === Notes === {{Footnote help}} {{reflist}} === References {{Wikicite|reference="Shima Shrine"|ref={{Harvid|Shima Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}}}. * {{Cite book|Japanese|editor={{jalink|Kenichi Tanigawa|Q2392596}} (ed.)|author=Maruyama Kii|publisher={{jalink|Hakusuisha|Q4346470}}|isbn=456002216X|ref={{Harvid|Kamayama Shrine and Shizuhi Shrine (Kami-gami)|1986}}}} * {{Cite book|Japanese|editor=Association for Shikinaisha Studies (ed.)|author=Kiyoshi Nakano|chapter=Shizuhi Shrine|year=1987|title=Report on Shikinaisha Surveys, Vol. 23 Nankaidō|publisher={{jalink|Kōgakkan University|Q1797584}} Press|page=|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Shizuhi Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987}}}} === Related items === * {{jalink|Kamayama Shrine|Q11597743}} {{Shinto sidebar}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Shizuhijinja}} == Categories == An extramural subsidiary shrine of {{ill|lt=Kamayama Shrine|Q11597743|WD=Q11597743}} |Founded = Unknown
(before {{ill|lt=Jōwa (Japan)|Q1075605|WD=Q1075605|lt=Jōwa}} 11 ({{ill|lt=844|Q23835|WD=Q23835}})) |Main Hall = |Alternate names = |Pilgrimage site = Ki Sansho-sha |Annual festival = {{ill|lt=April 16|Q2519|WD=Q2519}} |Rites = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Wakayama|label=Shizuhi Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=220}} }} {{Coordinate list}} [[File:Shizuhi-jinja kyuuchi.JPG|thumb|250px|right|Former site monument of Shizuhi Shrine (foreground) and Mount Yakushi, where the current shrine stands]] '''Shizuhi Shrine''' (しずひじんじゃ / しづひじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} located in Wada, {{ill|lt=Wakayama Prefecture|Q131314|WD=Q131314}}{{ill|lt=Wakayama City|Q200747|WD=Q200747}}. It is a {{ill||lt=Shikinaisha|UNKNOWN}} ({{ill|lt=Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964|WD=Q9610964}}), and under the {{ill|lt=modern system of shrine ranks|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=former shrine ranking system}} it was classified as a {{ill|lt=Village Shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284}}. Today, it is an extramural subsidiary shrine of {{ill|lt=Kamayama Shrine|Q11597743|WD=Q11597743}}. Together with {{ill|lt=Date Shrine (Wakayama City)|Q11380847|WD=Q11380847|lt=Date Shrine}} (Sonobe, Wakayama City) and {{ill|lt=Shima Shrine|Q17212913|WD=Q17212913}} ({{ill|lt=Nakanoshima (Wakayama City)|Q17226981|WD=Q17226981|lt=Nakanoshima}}, Wakayama City), it is known as the “Ki Sansho-sha” (Three Shrines of Ki) {{Sfn|Shizuhi Shrine Site (Heibonsha)|1983|p=376}}. === Enshrined deity === The enshrined deity is the following single kami {{Sfn|Shizuhi Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987|p=99}}: :* Shizuhi Ōkami According to documents of the Ugai clan, the shrine’s hereditary priests, the deity was identified as {{ill|lt=Kagutsuchi|Q1640658|WD=Q1640658|lt=Hi-no-Musubi no Kami}} {{Sfn|Shizuhi Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987|p=99}}. Another theory, found in the *Zoku Fudoki*, holds that the “Ki Sansho-sha” were branch enshrinements of the three deities of Isonokami—Itsu no Take no Mikoto, Ōyatsuhime no Mikoto, and Tsumatsuhime no Mikoto—and identifies the deity of Shizuhi Shrine as Tsumatsuhime no Mikoto {{Sfn|Shizuhi Shrine Site (Heibonsha)|1983|p=376}}. Yet another view considers Shizuhi to have been a “fire-quelling deity” of Kii Province, known as the “Land of Trees” {{Sfn|Kamayama Shrine and Shizuhi Shrine (Kami-gami)|1986|pp=316–318}}. In addition, the *{{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki|Q11381815|WD=Q11381815}}* states that Funatama Shrine, a subsidiary shrine of {{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi Taisha|Q705949|WD=Q705949}}, was the clan deity of the Ki clan and the main shrine of “Shima Shrine, Shizuhi Shrine, and Date Shrine.” === History === === Founding === The date of founding is unknown. Originally, the shrine stood about 200 meters east of its current location ({{Coord|34|12|9.46|N|135|12|37.07|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|name=Former site of Shizuhi Shrine}}) {{Sfn|Shizuhi Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987|p=99}}. According to the *Zoku Fudoki*, Shizuhi Shrine had fallen into ruin before the {{ill|lt=Einin|Q1327307|WD=Q1327307}} era ({{ill|lt=1293|Q5557|WD=Q5557}}–{{ill|lt=1299|Q5564|WD=Q5564}}), but a document of the Hinokuma Shrine from {{ill|lt=Chōkyō|Q968843|WD=Q968843}} 1 ({{ill|lt=1487|Q6652|WD=Q6652}}) records that one *to* of ritual rice was allocated to Shizuhi Shrine {{Sfn|Shizuhi Shrine Site (Heibonsha)|1983|p=376}}. According to the *Nanki Tokugawa-shi*, by around {{ill|lt=Kyōhō|Q1145048|WD=Q1145048}} 8 ({{ill|lt=1723|Q6921|WD=Q6921}}) the shrine had again fallen into ruin; in that same year, Wakayama Domain rebuilt the shrine buildings on Mount Yakushi (Maeyama / Amagiri-yama; the present site), and Kamayama Shrine assumed its administration {{Sfn|Shizuhi Shrine Site (Heibonsha)|1983|p=376}}. === Overview === In the national histories, together with Date Shrine and Shima Shrine (Shima Jinja), Shizuhi Shrine is recorded as having received promotions in divine rank: from Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade to Senior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade in {{ill|lt=Jōwa (Japan)|Q1075605|WD=Q1075605|lt=Jōwa}} 11 ({{ill|lt=844|Q23835|WD=Q23835}}); to Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade in {{ill|lt=Kashō|Q1373157|WD=Q1373157}} 3 ({{ill|lt=850|Q30364|WD=Q30364}}); to Senior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade in {{ill|lt=Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan}} 1 ({{ill|lt=859|Q23897|WD=Q23897}}); and to Junior Third Rank in Jōgan 17 ({{ill|lt=875|Q23816|WD=Q23816}}) {{Sfn|Shizuhi Shrine Site (Heibonsha)|1983|p=376}}. In the *{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448}}* *{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Register of Deities}}*, it is listed under {{ill|lt=Kii Province|Q864053|WD=Q864053}}{{ill|lt=Nagusa District|Q11415547|WD=Q11415547}} as “Shizuhi Shrine, Myōjin Grand Shrine,” placing it among the {{ill|lt=Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964|WD=Q9610964}}; the *{{ill|lt=Kii no Kuni Jinmyōchō|Q17210008|WD=Q17210008}}* records it as “Senior First Rank, Shizuhi Ōkami” {{Sfn|Shizuhi Shrine Site (Heibonsha)|1983|p=376}}. In all cases, it is ranked higher than Kamayama Shrine. It is said that together with {{ill|lt=Date Shrine (Wakayama City)|Q11380847|WD=Q11380847|lt=Date Shrine}} (Sonobe, Wakayama City) and {{ill|lt=Shima Shrine|Q17212913|WD=Q17212913}} ({{ill|lt=Nakanoshima (Wakayama City)|Q17226981|WD=Q17226981|lt=Nakanoshima}}, Wakayama City), it was known as the “Ki Sansho-sha” {{Sfn|Shima Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983|p=362}}. References to the Ki Sansho-sha appear beginning with a rice storage ledger from {{ill|lt=Eishō|Q1193549|WD=Q1193549}} 3 ({{ill|lt=1048|Q19359|WD=Q19359}}), as well as in the *{{ill|lt=Chūyūki|Q11363231|WD=Q11363231}}* entry for {{ill|lt=Tennen|Q1193599|WD=Q1193599}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1109|Q19515|WD=Q19515}}) and in the four-verse sacred songs of the *{{ill|lt=Ryōjin Hishō|Q3010584|WD=Q3010584}}* {{Sfn|Shima Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983|p=362}}. According to the *{{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki|Q11381815|WD=Q11381815}}*, the origin of the Ki Sansho-sha lies in the three ships used by {{ill|lt=Empress Jingū|Q232803|WD=Q232803}} for the {{ill|lt=conquest of the Three Kingdoms of Korea|Q11249435|WD=Q11249435}}, which she had {{ill|lt=Takenouchi no Sukune|Q1073668|WD=Q1073668}} enshrine {{Sfn|Shima Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983|p=362}}. Furthermore, since Wada Village was formerly a shrine estate of {{ill|lt=Hinokuma Shrine and Kunikakasu Shrine|Q888184|WD=Q888184}}, the deity of Shizuhi was closely connected with those shrines and with the {{ill|lt=Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Q11604953|WD=Q11604953|lt=hereditary governors of Kii Province}}. At Kusa-no-miya, enshrined within the grounds of those shrines, it is said that on September 15 the deity of Shizuhi made a sacred procession and the Shizuhi Festival was held {{Sfn|Kamayama Shrine and Shizuhi Shrine (Kami-gami)|1986|pp=316–318}}. In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757}}), under the {{ill|lt=modern system of shrine ranks|Q712534|WD=Q712534}}, it was designated a {{ill|lt=Village Shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284}}; however, in {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}} 42 ({{ill|lt=1909|Q2057|WD=Q2057}}) it was merged into Inari Shrine, a subsidiary shrine of Kamayama Shrine {{Sfn|Shizuhi Shrine Site (Heibonsha)|1983|p=376}}{{Sfn|Shizuhi Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987|p=99}}. In {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567}} 25 ({{ill|lt=1950|Q18597|WD=Q18597}}), shrine buildings were constructed on the original Mount Yakushi site and the deity was transferred there {{Sfn|Shizuhi Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987|p=99}}. === Divine ranks === * Records of divine rank promotions in the Six National Histories ** {{ill|lt=Jōwa (Japan)|Q1075605|WD=Q1075605|lt=Jōwa}} 11 ({{ill|lt=844|Q23835|WD=Q23835}}) 11th month, 3rd day: from Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade to Senior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade (*{{ill|lt=Shoku Nihon Kōki|Q1041301|WD=Q1041301}}*) – recorded as “Shizuhi no Kami.” ** {{ill|lt=Kashō|Q1373157|WD=Q1373157}} 3 ({{ill|lt=850|Q30364|WD=Q30364}}) 10th month, 21st day: Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade (*{{ill|lt=Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|Q1052905|WD=Q1052905}}*) – recorded as “Shizuhi no Kami.” ** {{ill|lt=Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan}} 1 ({{ill|lt=859|Q23897|WD=Q23897}}) 1st month, 27th day: from Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade to Senior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade (*{{ill|lt=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Q1052981|WD=Q1052981}}*) – recorded as “Shizuhi no Kami.” ** Jōgan 17 ({{ill|lt=875|Q23816|WD=Q23816}}) 10th month, 17th day: from Senior Fourth Rank, Upper Grade to Junior Third Rank (*Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku*) – recorded as “Shizuhi no Kami.” * After the Six National Histories ** Senior First Rank (*{{ill|lt=Kii no Kuni Jinmyōchō|Q17210008|WD=Q17210008}}*) – recorded as “Shizuhi Ōkami.” === Notes === {{Footnote help}} {{reflist}} === References === * Shrine historical records * {{Cite book|Japanese|editor=|author=|year=1983|chapter=|title={{ill|lt=Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|Q17213509|WD=Q17213509}} 31 Place Names of Wakayama Prefecture|publisher={{ill|lt=Heibonsha|Q11482108|WD=Q11482108}}|isbn=458249031X|ref=}} ** {{Wikicite|reference="Shizuhi Shrine Site"|ref={{Harvid|Shizuhi Shrine Site (Heibonsha)|1983}}}}, {{Wikicite|reference="Shima Shrine"|ref={{Harvid|Shima Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}}}. * {{Cite book|Japanese|editor={{ill|lt=Kenichi Tanigawa|Q2392596|WD=Q2392596}} (ed.)|author=Maruyama Akinori|year=1986|chapter=Kamayama Shrine and Shizuhi Shrine|title=Gods of Japan – Shrines and Sacred Places – Vol. 6 Ise, Shima, Iga, Kii|publisher={{ill|lt=Hakusuisha|Q4346470|WD=Q4346470}}|isbn=456002216X|ref={{Harvid|Kamayama Shrine and Shizuhi Shrine (Kami-gami)|1986}}}} * {{Cite book|Japanese|editor=Association for Shikinaisha Studies (ed.)|author=Kiyoshi Nakano|chapter=Shizuhi Shrine|year=1987|title=Report on Shikinaisha Surveys, Vol. 23 Nankaidō|publisher={{ill|lt=Kōgakkan University|Q1797584|WD=Q1797584}} Press|page=|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Shizuhi Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987}}}} === Related items === * {{ill|lt=Kamayama Shrine|Q11597743|WD=Q11597743}} {{Shinto sidebar}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Shizuhijinja}} == Shrine|en|Kamayama Shrine|fr|Sanctuaire Kamayama|ja|竈山神社|simple|Kamayama Shrine|WD=Q11597743}} {{commons category|Category:Shizuhi-jinja}} [[JA:静火神社]] {{wikidata link|Q28693031}} == Japanese content == {{Shinto shrine |Name = Shizuhi Shrine |Image = [[File:Shizuhi-jinja.JPG|250px]]
Torii gate and shrine buildings |Location = Wada, {{ill|lt=Wakayama Prefecture|Q131314|WD=Q131314}}{{ill|lt=Wakayama City|Q200747|WD=Q200747}} |Coordinates= {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|12|10.64|N|135|12|28.86|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Shizuhi Shrine}} |Deity = Shizuhi Ōkami |Shintai = |Rank = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|UNKNOWN}} ({{ill|lt=Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964|WD=Q9610964|lt=Myōjin Grand Shrine}})
Former {{ill|lt=Village Shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284}}
An extramural subsidiary shrine of {{ill|lt=Kamayama Shrine|Q11597743|WD=Q11597743}} |Founded = Unknown
(before {{ill|lt=Jōwa (Japan)|Q1075605|WD=Q1075605|lt=Jōwa}} 11 ({{ill|lt=844|Q23835|WD=Q23835}})) |Main Hall = |Alternate names = |Pilgrimage site = Ki Sansho-sha |Annual festival = {{ill|lt=April 16|Q2519|WD=Q2519}} |Rites = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Wakayama|label=Shizuhi Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=220}} }} {{Coordinate list}} [[File:Shizuhi-jinja kyuuchi.JPG|thumb|250px|right|Former site monument of Shizuhi Shrine (foreground) and Mount Yakushi, where the current shrine stands]] '''Shizuhi Shrine''' (しずひじんじゃ / しづひじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} located in Wada, {{ill|lt=Wakayama Prefecture|Q131314|WD=Q131314}}{{ill|lt=Wakayama City|Q200747|WD=Q200747}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|UNKNOWN}} ({{ill|lt=Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964|WD=Q9610964}}), and under the {{ill|lt=modern system of shrine ranks|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=former shrine ranking system}} it was classified as a {{ill|lt=Village Shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284}}. Today, it is an extramural subsidiary shrine of {{ill|lt=Kamayama Shrine|Q11597743|WD=Q11597743}}. Together with {{ill|lt=Date Shrine (Wakayama City)|Q11380847|WD=Q11380847|lt=Date Shrine}} (Sonobe, Wakayama City) and {{ill|lt=Shima Shrine|Q17212913|WD=Q17212913}} ({{ill|lt=Nakanoshima (Wakayama City)|Q17226981|WD=Q17226981|lt=Nakanoshima}}, Wakayama City), it is known as the “Ki Sansho-sha” (Three Shrines of Ki) {{Sfn|Shizuhi Shrine Site (Heibonsha)|1983|p=376}}. === Enshrined deity === The enshrined deity is the following single kami {{Sfn|Shizuhi Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987|p=99}}: :* Shizuhi Ōkami According to documents of the Ugai clan, the shrine’s hereditary priests, the deity was identified as {{ill|lt=Kagutsuchi|Q1640658|WD=Q1640658|lt=Hi-no-Musubi no Kami}} {{Sfn|Shizuhi Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987|p=99}}. Another theory, found in the *Zoku Fudoki*, holds that the “Ki Sansho-sha” were branch enshrinements of the three deities of Isonokami—Itsu no Take no Mikoto, Ōyatsuhime no Mikoto, and Tsumatsuhime no Mikoto—and identifies the deity of Shizuhi Shrine as Tsumatsuhime no Mikoto {{Sfn|Shizuhi Shrine Site (Heibonsha)|1983|p=376}}. Yet another view considers Shizuhi to have been a “fire-quelling deity” of Kii Province, known as the “Land of Trees” {{Sfn|Kamayama Shrine and Shizuhi Shrine (Kami-gami)|1986|pp=316–318}}. In addition, the *{{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki|Q11381815|WD=Q11381815}}* states that Funatama Shrine, a subsidiary shrine of {{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi Taisha|Q705949|WD=Q705949}}, was the clan deity of the Ki clan and the main shrine of “Shima Shrine, Shizuhi Shrine, and Date Shrine.” === History === === Founding === The date of founding is unknown. Originally, the shrine stood about 200 meters east of its current location ({{Coord|34|12|9.46|N|135|12|37.07|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|name=Former site of Shizuhi Shrine}}) {{Sfn|Shizuhi Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987|p=99}}. According to the *Zoku Fudoki*, Shizuhi Shrine had fallen into ruin before the {{ill|lt=Einin|Q1327307|WD=Q1327307}} era ({{ill|lt=1293|Q5557|WD=Q5557}}–{{ill|lt=1299|Q5564|WD=Q5564}}), but a document of the Hinokuma Shrine from {{ill|lt=Chōkyō|Q968843|WD=Q968843}} 1 ({{ill|lt=1487|Q6652|WD=Q6652}}) records that one *to* of ritual rice was allocated to Shizuhi Shrine {{Sfn|Shizuhi Shrine Site (Heibonsha)|1983|p=376}}. According to the *Nanki Tokugawa-shi*, by around {{ill|lt=Kyōhō|Q1145048|WD=Q1145048}} 8 ({{ill|lt=1723|Q6921|WD=Q6921}}) the shrine had again fallen into ruin; in that same year, Wakayama Domain rebuilt the shrine buildings on Mount Yakushi (Maeyama / Amagiri-yama; the present site), and Kamayama Shrine assumed its administration {{Sfn|Shizuhi Shrine Site (Heibonsha)|1983|p=376}}. === Overview === In the national histories, together with Date Shrine and Shima Shrine (Shima Jinja), Shizuhi Shrine is recorded as having received promotions in divine rank: from Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade to Senior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade in {{ill|lt=Jōwa (Japan)|Q1075605|WD=Q1075605|lt=Jōwa}} 11 ({{ill|lt=844|Q23835|WD=Q23835}}); to Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade in {{ill|lt=Kashō|Q1373157|WD=Q1373157}} 3 ({{ill|lt=850|Q30364|WD=Q30364}}); to Senior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade in {{ill|lt=Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan}} 1 ({{ill|lt=859|Q23897|WD=Q23897}}); and to Junior Third Rank in Jōgan 17 ({{ill|lt=875|Q23816|WD=Q23816}}) {{Sfn|Shizuhi Shrine Site (Heibonsha)|1983|p=376}}. In the *{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448}}* *{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Register of Deities}}*, it is listed under {{ill|lt=Kii Province|Q864053|WD=Q864053}}{{ill|lt=Nagusa District|Q11415547|WD=Q11415547}} as “Shizuhi Shrine, Myōjin Grand Shrine,” placing it among the {{ill|lt=Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964|WD=Q9610964}}; the *{{ill|lt=Kii no Kuni Jinmyōchō|Q17210008|WD=Q17210008}}* records it as “Senior First Rank, Shizuhi Ōkami” {{Sfn|Shizuhi Shrine Site (Heibonsha)|1983|p=376}}. In all cases, it is ranked higher than Kamayama Shrine. It is said that together with {{ill|lt=Date Shrine (Wakayama City)|Q11380847|WD=Q11380847|lt=Date Shrine}} (Sonobe, Wakayama City) and {{ill|lt=Shima Shrine|Q17212913|WD=Q17212913}} ({{ill|lt=Nakanoshima (Wakayama City)|Q17226981|WD=Q17226981|lt=Nakanoshima}}, Wakayama City), it was known as the “Ki Sansho-sha” {{Sfn|Shima Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983|p=362}}. References to the Ki Sansho-sha appear beginning with a rice storage ledger from {{ill|lt=Eishō|Q1193549|WD=Q1193549}} 3 ({{ill|lt=1048|Q19359|WD=Q19359}}), as well as in the *{{ill|lt=Chūyūki|Q11363231|WD=Q11363231}}* entry for {{ill|lt=Tennen|Q1193599|WD=Q1193599}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1109|Q19515|WD=Q19515}}) and in the four-verse sacred songs of the *{{ill|lt=Ryōjin Hishō|Q3010584|WD=Q3010584}}* {{Sfn|Shima Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983|p=362}}. According to the *{{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki|Q11381815|WD=Q11381815}}*, the origin of the Ki Sansho-sha lies in the three ships used by {{ill|lt=Empress Jingū|Q232803|WD=Q232803}} for the {{ill|lt=conquest of the Three Kingdoms of Korea|Q11249435|WD=Q11249435}}, which she had {{ill|lt=Takenouchi no Sukune|Q1073668|WD=Q1073668}} enshrine {{Sfn|Shima Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983|p=362}}. Furthermore, since Wada Village was formerly a shrine estate of {{ill|lt=Hinokuma Shrine and Kunikakasu Shrine|Q888184|WD=Q888184}}, the deity of Shizuhi was closely connected with those shrines and with the {{ill|lt=Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Q11604953|WD=Q11604953|lt=hereditary governors of Kii Province}}. At Kusa-no-miya, enshrined within the grounds of those shrines, it is said that on September 15 the deity of Shizuhi made a sacred procession and the Shizuhi Festival was held {{Sfn|Kamayama Shrine and Shizuhi Shrine (Kami-gami)|1986|pp=316–318}}. In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757}}), under the {{ill|lt=modern system of shrine ranks|Q712534|WD=Q712534}}, it was designated a {{ill|lt=Village Shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284}}; however, in {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}} 42 ({{ill|lt=1909|Q2057|WD=Q2057}}) it was merged into Inari Shrine, a subsidiary shrine of Kamayama Shrine {{Sfn|Shizuhi Shrine Site (Heibonsha)|1983|p=376}}{{Sfn|Shizuhi Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987|p=99}}. In {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567}} 25 ({{ill|lt=1950|Q18597|WD=Q18597}}), shrine buildings were constructed on the original Mount Yakushi site and the deity was transferred there {{Sfn|Shizuhi Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987|p=99}}. === Divine ranks === * Records of divine rank promotions in the Six National Histories ** {{ill|lt=Jōwa (Japan)|Q1075605|WD=Q1075605|lt=Jōwa}} 11 ({{ill|lt=844|Q23835|WD=Q23835}}) 11th month, 3rd day: from Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade to Senior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade (*{{ill|lt=Shoku Nihon Kōki|Q1041301|WD=Q1041301}}*) – recorded as “Shizuhi no Kami.” ** {{ill|lt=Kashō|Q1373157|WD=Q1373157}} 3 ({{ill|lt=850|Q30364|WD=Q30364}}) 10th month, 21st day: Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade (*{{ill|lt=Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|Q1052905|WD=Q1052905}}*) – recorded as “Shizuhi no Kami.” ** {{ill|lt=Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan}} 1 ({{ill|lt=859|Q23897|WD=Q23897}}) 1st month, 27th day: from Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade to Senior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade (*{{ill|lt=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Q1052981|WD=Q1052981}}*) – recorded as “Shizuhi no Kami.” ** Jōgan 17 ({{ill|lt=875|Q23816|WD=Q23816}}) 10th month, 17th day: from Senior Fourth Rank, Upper Grade to Junior Third Rank (*Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku*) – recorded as “Shizuhi no Kami.” * After the Six National Histories ** Senior First Rank (*{{ill|lt=Kii no Kuni Jinmyōchō|Q17210008|WD=Q17210008}}*) – recorded as “Shizuhi Ōkami.” === Notes === {{Footnote help}} {{reflist}} === References === * Shrine historical records * {{Cite book|Japanese|editor=|author=|year=1983|chapter=|title={{ill|lt=Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|Q17213509|WD=Q17213509}} 31 Place Names of Wakayama Prefecture|publisher={{ill|lt=Heibonsha|Q11482108|WD=Q11482108}}|isbn=458249031X|ref=}} ** {{Wikicite|reference="Shizuhi Shrine Site"|ref={{Harvid|Shizuhi Shrine Site (Heibonsha)|1983}}}}, {{Wikicite|reference="Shima Shrine"|ref={{Harvid|Shima Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}}}. * {{Cite book|Japanese|editor={{ill|lt=Kenichi Tanigawa|Q2392596|WD=Q2392596}} (ed.)|author=Maruyama Akinori|year=1986|chapter=Kamayama Shrine and Shizuhi Shrine|title=Gods of Japan – Shrines and Sacred Places – Vol. 6 Ise, Shima, Iga, Kii|publisher={{ill|lt=Hakusuisha|Q4346470|WD=Q4346470}}|isbn=456002216X|ref={{Harvid|Kamayama Shrine and Shizuhi Shrine (Kami-gami)|1986}}}} * {{Cite book|Japanese|editor=Association for Shikinaisha Studies (ed.)|author=Kiyoshi Nakano|chapter=Shizuhi Shrine|year=1987|title=Report on Shikinaisha Surveys, Vol. 23 Nankaidō|publisher={{ill|lt=Kōgakkan University|Q1797584|WD=Q1797584}} Press|page=|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Shizuhi Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987}}}} === Related items === * {{ill|lt=Kamayama Shrine|Q11597743|WD=Q11597743}} {{Shinto sidebar}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Shizuhijinja}} == Shrine|en|Kamayama Shrine|fr|Sanctuaire Kamayama|ja|竈山神社|simple|Kamayama Shrine|WD=Q11597743}} {{commons category|Category:Shizuhi-jinja}} [[JA:静火神社]] {{wikidata link|Q28693031}} == Japanese content == {{Shinto shrine |Name = Shizuhi Shrine |Image = [[File:Shizuhi-jinja.JPG|250px]]
Torii gate and shrine buildings |Location = Wada, {{ill|lt=Wakayama Prefecture|Q131314|WD=Q131314}}{{ill|lt=Wakayama City|Q200747|WD=Q200747}} |Coordinates= {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|12|10.64|N|135|12|28.86|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Shizuhi Shrine}} |Deity = Shizuhi Ōkami |Shintai = |Rank = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN}} ({{ill|lt=Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964|WD=Q9610964|lt=Myōjin Grand Shrine}})
Former {{ill|lt=Village Shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284}}
An extramural subsidiary shrine of {{ill|lt=Kamayama Shrine|Q11597743|WD=Q11597743}} |Founded = Unknown
(before {{ill|lt=Jōwa (Japan)|Q1075605|WD=Q1075605|lt=Jōwa}} 11 ({{ill|lt=844|Q23835|WD=Q23835}})) |Main Hall = |Alternate names = |Pilgrimage site = Ki Sansho-sha |Annual festival = {{ill|lt=April 16|Q2519|WD=Q2519}} |Rites = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Wakayama|label=Shizuhi Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=220}} }} {{Coordinate list}} [[File:Shizuhi-jinja kyuuchi.JPG|thumb|250px|right|Former site monument of Shizuhi Shrine (foreground) and Mount Yakushi, where the current shrine stands]] '''Shizuhi Shrine''' (しずひじんじゃ / しづひじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} located in Wada, {{ill|lt=Wakayama Prefecture|Q131314|WD=Q131314}}{{ill|lt=Wakayama City|Q200747|WD=Q200747}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN}} ({{ill|lt=Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964|WD=Q9610964}}), and under the {{ill|lt=modern system of shrine ranks|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=former shrine ranking system}} it was classified as a {{ill|lt=Village Shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284}}. Today, it is an extramural subsidiary shrine of {{ill|lt=Kamayama Shrine|Q11597743|WD=Q11597743}}. Together with {{ill|lt=Date Shrine (Wakayama City)|Q11380847|WD=Q11380847|lt=Date Shrine}} (Sonobe, Wakayama City) and {{ill|lt=Shima Shrine|Q17212913|WD=Q17212913}} ({{ill|lt=Nakanoshima (Wakayama City)|Q17226981|WD=Q17226981|lt=Nakanoshima}}, Wakayama City), it is known as the “Ki Sansho-sha” (Three Shrines of Ki) {{Sfn|Shizuhi Shrine Site (Heibonsha)|1983|p=376}}. === Enshrined deity === The enshrined deity is the following single kami {{Sfn|Shizuhi Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987|p=99}}: :* Shizuhi Ōkami According to documents of the Ugai clan, the shrine’s hereditary priests, the deity was identified as {{ill|lt=Kagutsuchi|Q1640658|WD=Q1640658|lt=Hi-no-Musubi no Kami}} {{Sfn|Shizuhi Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987|p=99}}. Another theory, found in the *Zoku Fudoki*, holds that the “Ki Sansho-sha” were branch enshrinements of the three deities of Isonokami—Itsu no Take no Mikoto, Ōyatsuhime no Mikoto, and Tsumatsuhime no Mikoto—and identifies the deity of Shizuhi Shrine as Tsumatsuhime no Mikoto {{Sfn|Shizuhi Shrine Site (Heibonsha)|1983|p=376}}. Yet another view considers Shizuhi to have been a “fire-quelling deity” of Kii Province, known as the “Land of Trees” {{Sfn|Kamayama Shrine and Shizuhi Shrine (Kami-gami)|1986|pp=316–318}}. In addition, the *{{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki|Q11381815|WD=Q11381815}}* states that Funatama Shrine, a subsidiary shrine of {{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi Taisha|Q705949|WD=Q705949}}, was the clan deity of the Ki clan and the main shrine of “Shima Shrine, Shizuhi Shrine, and Date Shrine.” === History === === Founding === The date of founding is unknown. Originally, the shrine stood about 200 meters east of its current location ({{Coord|34|12|9.46|N|135|12|37.07|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|name=Former site of Shizuhi Shrine}}) {{Sfn|Shizuhi Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987|p=99}}. According to the *Zoku Fudoki*, Shizuhi Shrine had fallen into ruin before the {{ill|lt=Einin|Q1327307|WD=Q1327307}} era ({{ill|lt=1293|Q5557|WD=Q5557}}–{{ill|lt=1299|Q5564|WD=Q5564}}), but a document of the Hinokuma Shrine from {{ill|lt=Chōkyō|Q968843|WD=Q968843}} 1 ({{ill|lt=1487|Q6652|WD=Q6652}}) records that one *to* of ritual rice was allocated to Shizuhi Shrine {{Sfn|Shizuhi Shrine Site (Heibonsha)|1983|p=376}}. According to the *Nanki Tokugawa-shi*, by around {{ill|lt=Kyōhō|Q1145048|WD=Q1145048}} 8 ({{ill|lt=1723|Q6921|WD=Q6921}}) the shrine had again fallen into ruin; in that same year, Wakayama Domain rebuilt the shrine buildings on Mount Yakushi (Maeyama / Amagiri-yama; the present site), and Kamayama Shrine assumed its administration {{Sfn|Shizuhi Shrine Site (Heibonsha)|1983|p=376}}. === Overview === In the national histories, together with Date Shrine and Shima Shrine (Shima Jinja), Shizuhi Shrine is recorded as having received promotions in divine rank: from Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade to Senior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade in {{ill|lt=Jōwa (Japan)|Q1075605|WD=Q1075605|lt=Jōwa}} 11 ({{ill|lt=844|Q23835|WD=Q23835}}); to Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade in {{ill|lt=Kashō|Q1373157|WD=Q1373157}} 3 ({{ill|lt=850|Q30364|WD=Q30364}}); to Senior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade in {{ill|lt=Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan}} 1 ({{ill|lt=859|Q23897|WD=Q23897}}); and to Junior Third Rank in Jōgan 17 ({{ill|lt=875|Q23816|WD=Q23816}}) {{Sfn|Shizuhi Shrine Site (Heibonsha)|1983|p=376}}. In the *{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448}}* *{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Register of Deities}}*, it is listed under {{ill|lt=Kii Province|Q864053|WD=Q864053}}{{ill|lt=Nagusa District|Q11415547|WD=Q11415547}} as “Shizuhi Shrine, Myōjin Grand Shrine,” placing it among the {{ill|lt=Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964|WD=Q9610964}}; the *{{ill|lt=Kii no Kuni Jinmyōchō|Q17210008|WD=Q17210008}}* records it as “Senior First Rank, Shizuhi Ōkami” {{Sfn|Shizuhi Shrine Site (Heibonsha)|1983|p=376}}. In all cases, it is ranked higher than Kamayama Shrine. It is said that together with {{ill|lt=Date Shrine (Wakayama City)|Q11380847|WD=Q11380847|lt=Date Shrine}} (Sonobe, Wakayama City) and {{ill|lt=Shima Shrine|Q17212913|WD=Q17212913}} ({{ill|lt=Nakanoshima (Wakayama City)|Q17226981|WD=Q17226981|lt=Nakanoshima}}, Wakayama City), it was known as the “Ki Sansho-sha” {{Sfn|Shima Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983|p=362}}. References to the Ki Sansho-sha appear beginning with a rice storage ledger from {{ill|lt=Eishō|Q1193549|WD=Q1193549}} 3 ({{ill|lt=1048|Q19359|WD=Q19359}}), as well as in the *{{ill|lt=Chūyūki|Q11363231|WD=Q11363231}}* entry for {{ill|lt=Tennen|Q1193599|WD=Q1193599}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1109|Q19515|WD=Q19515}}) and in the four-verse sacred songs of the *{{ill|lt=Ryōjin Hishō|Q3010584|WD=Q3010584}}* {{Sfn|Shima Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983|p=362}}. According to the *{{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki|Q11381815|WD=Q11381815}}*, the origin of the Ki Sansho-sha lies in the three ships used by {{ill|lt=Empress Jingū|Q232803|WD=Q232803}} for the {{ill|lt=conquest of the Three Kingdoms of Korea|Q11249435|WD=Q11249435}}, which she had {{ill|lt=Takenouchi no Sukune|Q1073668|WD=Q1073668}} enshrine {{Sfn|Shima Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983|p=362}}. Furthermore, since Wada Village was formerly a shrine estate of {{ill|lt=Hinokuma Shrine and Kunikakasu Shrine|Q888184|WD=Q888184}}, the deity of Shizuhi was closely connected with those shrines and with the {{ill|lt=Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Q11604953|WD=Q11604953|lt=hereditary governors of Kii Province}}. At Kusa-no-miya, enshrined within the grounds of those shrines, it is said that on September 15 the deity of Shizuhi made a sacred procession and the Shizuhi Festival was held {{Sfn|Kamayama Shrine and Shizuhi Shrine (Kami-gami)|1986|pp=316–318}}. In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757}}), under the {{ill|lt=modern system of shrine ranks|Q712534|WD=Q712534}}, it was designated a {{ill|lt=Village Shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284}}; however, in {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}} 42 ({{ill|lt=1909|Q2057|WD=Q2057}}) it was merged into Inari Shrine, a subsidiary shrine of Kamayama Shrine {{Sfn|Shizuhi Shrine Site (Heibonsha)|1983|p=376}}{{Sfn|Shizuhi Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987|p=99}}. In {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567}} 25 ({{ill|lt=1950|Q18597|WD=Q18597}}), shrine buildings were constructed on the original Mount Yakushi site and the deity was transferred there {{Sfn|Shizuhi Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987|p=99}}. === Divine ranks === * Records of divine rank promotions in the Six National Histories ** {{ill|lt=Jōwa (Japan)|Q1075605|WD=Q1075605|lt=Jōwa}} 11 ({{ill|lt=844|Q23835|WD=Q23835}}) 11th month, 3rd day: from Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade to Senior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade (*{{ill|lt=Shoku Nihon Kōki|Q1041301|WD=Q1041301}}*) – recorded as “Shizuhi no Kami.” ** {{ill|lt=Kashō|Q1373157|WD=Q1373157}} 3 ({{ill|lt=850|Q30364|WD=Q30364}}) 10th month, 21st day: Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade (*{{ill|lt=Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|Q1052905|WD=Q1052905}}*) – recorded as “Shizuhi no Kami.” ** {{ill|lt=Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan}} 1 ({{ill|lt=859|Q23897|WD=Q23897}}) 1st month, 27th day: from Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade to Senior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade (*{{ill|lt=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Q1052981|WD=Q1052981}}*) – recorded as “Shizuhi no Kami.” ** Jōgan 17 ({{ill|lt=875|Q23816|WD=Q23816}}) 10th month, 17th day: from Senior Fourth Rank, Upper Grade to Junior Third Rank (*Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku*) – recorded as “Shizuhi no Kami.” * After the Six National Histories ** Senior First Rank (*{{ill|lt=Kii no Kuni Jinmyōchō|Q17210008|WD=Q17210008}}*) – recorded as “Shizuhi Ōkami.” === Notes === {{Footnote help}} {{reflist}} === References === * Shrine historical records * {{Cite Shrine|en|Kamayama Shrine|fr|Sanctuaire Kamayama|ja|竈山神社|simple|Kamayama Shrine|WD=Q11597743}} {{commons category|Category:Shizuhi-jinja}} [[JA:静火神社]] {{wikidata link|Q28693031}} == Japanese content == {{Shinto shrine |Name = Shizuhi Shrine |Image = [[File:Shizuhi-jinja.JPG|250px]]
Torii gate and shrine buildings |Location = Wada, {{ill|lt=Wakayama Prefecture|Q131314|WD=Q131314}}{{ill|lt=Wakayama City|Q200747|WD=Q200747}} |Coordinates= {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|12|10.64|N|135|12|28.86|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Shizuhi Shrine}} |Deity = Shizuhi Ōkami |Shintai = |Rank = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}} ({{ill|lt=Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964|WD=Q9610964|lt=Myōjin Grand Shrine}})
Former {{ill|lt=Village Shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284}}
An extramural subsidiary shrine of {{ill|lt=Kamayama Shrine|Q11597743|WD=Q11597743}} |Founded = Unknown
(before {{ill|lt=Jōwa (Japan)|Q1075605|WD=Q1075605|lt=Jōwa}} 11 ({{ill|lt=844|Q23835|WD=Q23835}})) |Main Hall = |Alternate names = |Pilgrimage site = Ki Sansho-sha |Annual festival = {{ill|lt=April 16|Q2519|WD=Q2519}} |Rites = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Wakayama|label=Shizuhi Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=220}} }} {{Coordinate list}} [[File:Shizuhi-jinja kyuuchi.JPG|thumb|250px|right|Former site monument of Shizuhi Shrine (foreground) and Mount Yakushi, where the current shrine stands]] '''Shizuhi Shrine''' (しずひじんじゃ / しづひじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} located in Wada, {{ill|lt=Wakayama Prefecture|Q131314|WD=Q131314}}{{ill|lt=Wakayama City|Q200747|WD=Q200747}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}} ({{ill|lt=Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964|WD=Q9610964}}), and under the {{ill|lt=modern system of shrine ranks|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=former shrine ranking system}} it was classified as a {{ill|lt=Village Shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284}}. Today, it is an extramural subsidiary shrine of {{ill|lt=Kamayama Shrine|Q11597743|WD=Q11597743}}. Together with {{ill|lt=Date Shrine (Wakayama City)|Q11380847|WD=Q11380847|lt=Date Shrine}} (Sonobe, Wakayama City) and {{ill|lt=Shima Shrine|Q17212913|WD=Q17212913}} ({{ill|lt=Nakanoshima (Wakayama City)|Q17226981|WD=Q17226981|lt=Nakanoshima}}, Wakayama City), it is known as the “Ki Sansho-sha” (Three Shrines of Ki) {{Sfn|Shizuhi Shrine Site (Heibonsha)|1983|p=376}}. === Enshrined deity === The enshrined deity is the following single kami {{Sfn|Shizuhi Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987|p=99}}: :* Shizuhi Ōkami According to documents of the Ugai clan, the shrine’s hereditary priests, the deity was identified as {{ill|lt=Kagutsuchi|Q1640658|WD=Q1640658|lt=Hi-no-Musubi no Kami}} {{Sfn|Shizuhi Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987|p=99}}. Another theory, found in the *Zoku Fudoki*, holds that the “Ki Sansho-sha” were branch enshrinements of the three deities of Isonokami—Itsu no Take no Mikoto, Ōyatsuhime no Mikoto, and Tsumatsuhime no Mikoto—and identifies the deity of Shizuhi Shrine as Tsumatsuhime no Mikoto {{Sfn|Shizuhi Shrine Site (Heibonsha)|1983|p=376}}. Yet another view considers Shizuhi to have been a “fire-quelling deity” of Kii Province, known as the “Land of Trees” {{Sfn|Kamayama Shrine and Shizuhi Shrine (Kami-gami)|1986|pp=316–318}}. In addition, the *{{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki|Q11381815|WD=Q11381815}}* states that Funatama Shrine, a subsidiary shrine of {{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi Taisha|Q705949|WD=Q705949}}, was the clan deity of the Ki clan and the main shrine of “Shima Shrine, Shizuhi Shrine, and Date Shrine.” === History === === Founding === The date of founding is unknown. Originally, the shrine stood about 200 meters east of its current location ({{Coord|34|12|9.46|N|135|12|37.07|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|name=Former site of Shizuhi Shrine}}) {{Sfn|Shizuhi Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987|p=99}}. According to the *Zoku Fudoki*, Shizuhi Shrine had fallen into ruin before the {{ill|lt=Einin|Q1327307|WD=Q1327307}} era ({{ill|lt=1293|Q5557|WD=Q5557}}–{{ill|lt=1299|Q5564|WD=Q5564}}), but a document of the Hinokuma Shrine from {{ill|lt=Chōkyō|Q968843|WD=Q968843}} 1 ({{ill|lt=1487|Q6652|WD=Q6652}}) records that one *to* of ritual rice was allocated to Shizuhi Shrine {{Sfn|Shizuhi Shrine Site (Heibonsha)|1983|p=376}}. According to the *Nanki Tokugawa-shi*, by around {{ill|lt=Kyōhō|Q1145048|WD=Q1145048}} 8 ({{ill|lt=1723|Q6921|WD=Q6921}}) the shrine had again fallen into ruin; in that same year, Wakayama Domain rebuilt the shrine buildings on Mount Yakushi (Maeyama / Amagiri-yama; the present site), and Kamayama Shrine assumed its administration {{Sfn|Shizuhi Shrine Site (Heibonsha)|1983|p=376}}. === Overview === In the national histories, together with Date Shrine and Shima Shrine (Shima Jinja), Shizuhi Shrine is recorded as having received promotions in divine rank: from Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade to Senior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade in {{ill|lt=Jōwa (Japan)|Q1075605|WD=Q1075605|lt=Jōwa}} 11 ({{ill|lt=844|Q23835|WD=Q23835}}); to Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade in {{ill|lt=Kashō|Q1373157|WD=Q1373157}} 3 ({{ill|lt=850|Q30364|WD=Q30364}}); to Senior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade in {{ill|lt=Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan}} 1 ({{ill|lt=859|Q23897|WD=Q23897}}); and to Junior Third Rank in Jōgan 17 ({{ill|lt=875|Q23816|WD=Q23816}}) {{Sfn|Shizuhi Shrine Site (Heibonsha)|1983|p=376}}. In the *{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448}}* *{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Register of Deities}}*, it is listed under {{ill|lt=Kii Province|Q864053|WD=Q864053}}{{ill|lt=Nagusa District|Q11415547|WD=Q11415547}} as “Shizuhi Shrine, Myōjin Grand Shrine,” placing it among the {{ill|lt=Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964|WD=Q9610964}}; the *{{ill|lt=Kii no Kuni Jinmyōchō|Q17210008|WD=Q17210008}}* records it as “Senior First Rank, Shizuhi Ōkami” {{Sfn|Shizuhi Shrine Site (Heibonsha)|1983|p=376}}. In all cases, it is ranked higher than Kamayama Shrine. It is said that together with {{ill|lt=Date Shrine (Wakayama City)|Q11380847|WD=Q11380847|lt=Date Shrine}} (Sonobe, Wakayama City) and {{ill|lt=Shima Shrine|Q17212913|WD=Q17212913}} ({{ill|lt=Nakanoshima (Wakayama City)|Q17226981|WD=Q17226981|lt=Nakanoshima}}, Wakayama City), it was known as the “Ki Sansho-sha” {{Sfn|Shima Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983|p=362}}. References to the Ki Sansho-sha appear beginning with a rice storage ledger from {{ill|lt=Eishō|Q1193549|WD=Q1193549}} 3 ({{ill|lt=1048|Q19359|WD=Q19359}}), as well as in the *{{ill|lt=Chūyūki|Q11363231|WD=Q11363231}}* entry for {{ill|lt=Tennen|Q1193599|WD=Q1193599}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1109|Q19515|WD=Q19515}}) and in the four-verse sacred songs of the *{{ill|lt=Ryōjin Hishō|Q3010584|WD=Q3010584}}* {{Sfn|Shima Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983|p=362}}. According to the *{{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi Taisha Jindaiki|Q11381815|WD=Q11381815}}*, the origin of the Ki Sansho-sha lies in the three ships used by {{ill|lt=Empress Jingū|Q232803|WD=Q232803}} for the {{ill|lt=conquest of the Three Kingdoms of Korea|Q11249435|WD=Q11249435}}, which she had {{ill|lt=Takenouchi no Sukune|Q1073668|WD=Q1073668}} enshrine {{Sfn|Shima Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983|p=362}}. Furthermore, since Wada Village was formerly a shrine estate of {{ill|lt=Hinokuma Shrine and Kunikakasu Shrine|Q888184|WD=Q888184}}, the deity of Shizuhi was closely connected with those shrines and with the {{ill|lt=Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Q11604953|WD=Q11604953|lt=hereditary governors of Kii Province}}. At Kusa-no-miya, enshrined within the grounds of those shrines, it is said that on September 15 the deity of Shizuhi made a sacred procession and the Shizuhi Festival was held {{Sfn|Kamayama Shrine and Shizuhi Shrine (Kami-gami)|1986|pp=316–318}}. In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757}}), under the {{ill|lt=modern system of shrine ranks|Q712534|WD=Q712534}}, it was designated a {{ill|lt=Village Shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284}}; however, in {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}} 42 ({{ill|lt=1909|Q2057|WD=Q2057}}) it was merged into Inari Shrine, a subsidiary shrine of Kamayama Shrine {{Sfn|Shizuhi Shrine Site (Heibonsha)|1983|p=376}}{{Sfn|Shizuhi Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987|p=99}}. In {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567}} 25 ({{ill|lt=1950|Q18597|WD=Q18597}}), shrine buildings were constructed on the original Mount Yakushi site and the deity was transferred there {{Sfn|Shizuhi Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987|p=99}}. === Divine ranks === * Records of divine rank promotions in the Six National Histories ** {{ill|lt=Jōwa (Japan)|Q1075605|WD=Q1075605|lt=Jōwa}} 11 ({{ill|lt=844|Q23835|WD=Q23835}}) 11th month, 3rd day: from Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade to Senior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade (*{{ill|lt=Shoku Nihon Kōki|Q1041301|WD=Q1041301}}*) – recorded as “Shizuhi no Kami.” ** {{ill|lt=Kashō|Q1373157|WD=Q1373157}} 3 ({{ill|lt=850|Q30364|WD=Q30364}}) 10th month, 21st day: Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade (*{{ill|lt=Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|Q1052905|WD=Q1052905}}*) – recorded as “Shizuhi no Kami.” ** {{ill|lt=Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan}} 1 ({{ill|lt=859|Q23897|WD=Q23897}}) 1st month, 27th day: from Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade to Senior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade (*{{ill|lt=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Q1052981|WD=Q1052981}}*) – recorded as “Shizuhi no Kami.” ** Jōgan 17 ({{ill|lt=875|Q23816|WD=Q23816}}) 10th month, 17th day: from Senior Fourth Rank, Upper Grade to Junior Third Rank (*Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku*) – recorded as “Shizuhi no Kami.” * After the Six National Histories ** Senior First Rank (*{{ill|lt=Kii no Kuni Jinmyōchō|Q17210008|WD=Q17210008}}*) – recorded as “Shizuhi Ōkami.” === Notes === {{Footnote help}} {{reflist}} === References === * Shrine historical records * {{Cite book|Japanese|editor=|author=|year=1983|chapter=|title={{ill|lt=Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|Q17213509|WD=Q17213509}} 31 Place Names of Wakayama Prefecture|publisher={{ill|lt=Heibonsha|Q11482108|WD=Q11482108}}|isbn=458249031X|ref=}} ** {{Wikicite|reference="Shizuhi Shrine Site"|ref={{Harvid|Shizuhi Shrine Site (Heibonsha)|1983}}}}, {{Wikicite|reference="Shima Shrine"|ref={{Harvid|Shima Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}}}. * {{Cite book|Japanese|editor={{ill|lt=Kenichi Tanigawa|Q2392596|WD=Q2392596}} (ed.)|author=Maruyama Akinori|year=1986|chapter=Kamayama Shrine and Shizuhi Shrine|title=Gods of Japan – Shrines and Sacred Places – Vol. 6 Ise, Shima, Iga, Kii|publisher={{ill|lt=Hakusuisha|Q4346470|WD=Q4346470}}|isbn=456002216X|ref={{Harvid|Kamayama Shrine and Shizuhi Shrine (Kami-gami)|1986}}}} * {{Cite book|Japanese|editor=Association for Shikinaisha Studies (ed.)|author=Kiyoshi Nakano|chapter=Shizuhi Shrine|year=1987|title=Report on Shikinaisha Surveys, Vol. 23 Nankaidō|publisher={{ill|lt=Kōgakkan University|Q1797584|WD=Q1797584}} Press|page=|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Shizuhi Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987}}}} === Related items === * {{ill|lt=Kamayama Shrine|Q11597743|WD=Q11597743}} {{Shinto sidebar}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Shizuhijinja}} ==
